name: Temple of Solomon
description: The Temple of Solomon, also known as the First Temple, held immense religious and historical significance in ancient Israel. Constructed in Jerusalem during the reign of King Solomon in the 10th century BCE, it served as the primary center for Israelite worship and a repository for the Ark of the Covenant. The Temple was not merely a place of worship; it symbolized the divine presence of Yahweh among the Israelites and represented the culmination of their covenant with God. The architectural design was said to be divinely inspired, and its construction involved vast resources and skilled artisans. The Temple Mount, where it stood, became the holiest site in Judaism. Its eventual destruction by the Babylonians in 586 BCE marked a profound turning point in Jewish history, leading to the Babylonian exile and the longing for its eventual restoration.
subtitle: The First Holy Temple in Jerusalem.
significance: Center of Israelite worship, dwelling place of Yahweh, symbol of the covenant between God and Israel.

keyMyths.title: The Construction of the Temple
keyMyths.description: According to biblical accounts, King Solomon received divine guidance in constructing the Temple. Skilled artisans, including Hiram of Tyre, were employed to build the Temple using cedar wood, precious stones, and gold. The construction was a monumental undertaking, requiring immense resources and labor, and it solidified Solomon's reign and the kingdom's prosperity. The dedication ceremony was a grand event with sacrifices and prayers.

keyMyths.title: The Ark of the Covenant
keyMyths.description: The Ark of the Covenant, containing the Ten Commandments, was housed in the Holy of Holies, the innermost sanctuary of the Temple. It represented God's presence and was considered the most sacred object in Israel. Only the High Priest could enter the Holy of Holies, and then only on the Day of Atonement. The Ark's presence sanctified the Temple and made it the focal point of Israelite worship.

keyMyths.title: The Destruction by Nebuchadnezzar
keyMyths.description: In 586 BCE, Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon conquered Jerusalem and destroyed the Temple. This event marked a catastrophic turning point in Jewish history, leading to the Babylonian exile. The destruction of the Temple symbolized the loss of God's favor and the shattering of Israel's national identity. The exile was a period of profound reflection and longing for the restoration of the Temple and the return to the land.

sources.author: Josephus
sources.description: Provides a detailed account of the Temple's construction and its role in Jewish society from a first-century CE perspective.
sources.text: Provides a detailed account of the Temple's construction and its role in Jewish society from a first-century CE perspective.

sources.author: Carol Meyers
sources.work: The Oxford History of the Biblical World
sources.description: Offers archaeological and historical context for understanding the Temple and its place in the ancient Near East.
sources.text: Offers archaeological and historical context for understanding the Temple and its place in the ancient Near East.

sources.author: Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman
sources.work: The Bible Unearthed: Archaeology's New Vision of Ancient Israel and the Origin of Its Sacred Texts
sources.description: Presents an archaeological perspective on the Temple, questioning some traditional interpretations.
sources.text: Presents an archaeological perspective on the Temple, questioning some traditional interpretations.
